questions.question: Explain the concept of function call overhead in C++ and how inline functions address this issue.
keyPoints: Function call overhead includes saving registers, saving stack pointer, passing arguments, jumping to function code, executing, and returning
This overhead occurs every time a function is called
Inline functions address this by inserting the function code directly at the call site during compilation
This eliminates the jump and return operations, reducing execution time

questions.question: What are the criteria for a good inline function candidate? Give examples of functions that should NOT be inlined.
keyPoints: Good candidates: small functions (1-3 lines), simple operations, frequently called functions, getter/setter functions
Functions with loops should not be inlined
Recursive functions should not be inlined
Large functions (more than 5-10 lines) cause code bloat
Virtual functions use dynamic binding so not reliably inlineable

questions.question: What is code bloat? How can excessive use of inline functions lead to code bloat?
keyPoints: Code bloat is the increase in executable size due to inline functions
When a function is inlined, its code is copied at every call site
If the same inline function is called 100 times, 100 copies of the same code appear in the binary
This increases memory usage and can hurt cache performance
Balance is needed between performance gain and code size

questions.question: Differentiate between inline functions and macros in C++. Why are inline functions preferred over macros?
keyPoints: Macros are preprocessor text substitutions with no type-checking
Inline functions are proper C++ functions with compiler type-checking
Macros can cause unexpected results due to side effects (e.g., macros with expressions)
Inline functions follow C++ scope and access rules
Inline functions can be debugged while macros cannot
Inline functions support object-oriented features

questions.question: Why are member functions defined inside a class automatically considered inline? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Member functions defined inside class declaration are implicitly inline
This is because the complete function definition is visible to the compiler in every translation unit that includes the header
Example: class MyClass { void display() { cout << "Hello"; } }; // automatically inline
The compiler needs the function body to generate code at each call site
This provides better performance for small member functions automatically

questions.question: Explain why virtual functions cannot be reliably inlined by the C++ compiler.
keyPoints: Virtual functions use dynamic binding (runtime polymorphism)
The compiler cannot determine at compile time which version of the virtual function will be called
The actual function to call is resolved at runtime using vtable
For inlining, the compiler needs to know the exact function to call at compile time
Virtual functions require runtime resolution, making inline optimization difficult

questions.question: What is the role of the 'inline' keyword? Is it a mandate or a suggestion to the compiler? Give reasons.
keyPoints: The 'inline' keyword is used to suggest the compiler to treat a function as inline
It is NOT a mandate - the compiler may ignore the suggestion
Compiler decides based on function size, complexity, optimization settings
If ignored, the function works as a normal function
Modern compilers may even inline functions not marked with 'inline' for optimization

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating inline functions. Include a function to calculate square of a number and a recursive function to calculate factorial. Explain which should be inlined and why.
keyPoints: Program with inline square function: inline int square(int x) { return x * x; }
Recursive factorial should NOT be inlined due to recursion
Demonstrate usage in main()
Explain: Square is small and called frequently - good candidate
Explain: Factorial uses recursion - cannot determine depth at compile time
Show compiler may ignore inline for factorial
Include proper headers and using namespace

questions.question: Explain the concept of One Definition Rule (ODR) and how inline functions are exempt from it. Why are inline functions typically placed in header files?
keyPoints: ODR states that entities with external linkage must have exactly one definition across all translation units
Inline functions are exempt - they can have identical definitions in multiple .cpp files
This is because each translation unit needs the function body to inline it
Inline functions in headers allow inclusion in multiple translation units
Without inline, this would cause linker errors (multiple definitions)
Header ensures every translation unit has access to the definition

questions.question: Compare normal function calls with inline function calls in terms of: (a) Execution mechanism (b) Memory usage (c) Compilation process (d) Debugging capability. Provide a suitable C++ code example to illustrate.
keyPoints: Execution: Normal - jump and return, Inline - code inserted at call site
Memory: Normal - single copy in binary, Inline - multiple copies at each call site
Compilation: Normal - separate compilation possible, Inline - needs definition at compile time
Debugging: Normal - easier to debug, Inline - difficult as no function call exists
Include code example showing both approaches
Demonstrate practical implications

questions.question: Design and implement a C++ class with inline member functions demonstrating good and bad inlining practices. Explain the decisions made for each function.
keyPoints: Create a class with multiple member functions
Good inline: getters, setters, simple calculations (1-2 lines)
Bad inline: functions with loops, large functions, complex logic
Show both inline keyword and implicit inline (defined inside class)
Explain why each choice was made
Include main() to demonstrate usage
Discuss compiler's discretion and code bloat implications
